reasoning:
The Bulgargaz-BOTAŞ agreement, signed for 13 years in 2023, is severely detrimental to Bulgaria, costing roughly €500k per day for unused capacity. This has led to massive financial losses, putting Bulgargaz on the verge of bankruptcy. In May 2024, the Bulgarian parliament legally instructed the Energy Minister to renegotiate the deal, and political leader Boyko Borissov directly urged Turkish President Erdogan to allow for a mutual cancellation. Furthermore, the contract is under EU Commission scrutiny for potentially violating European competition laws.

However, terminating the agreement is highly complex. The contract reportedly lacks a standard termination clause. Unilateral termination by Bulgaria could trigger an ICC arbitration claim from Turkey for the entirely of the contract's anticipated revenue (~€1.4 to €2.1 billion). Because the penalty for canceling is almost as high as the cost of keeping it, Bulgaria faces a massive barrier to simply walking away, making a drawn-out renegotiation or prolonged default more likely than an explicit, immediate, and formal "cancellation." 

Yet, there are legitimate pathways to a formal cancellation before July 1, 2025 (a ~13-month horizon from the latest May 2024 events). The EU Commission could rule the contract invalid under EU law, providing Bulgaria a legal mandate (and shield) to formally terminate it. Alternatively, Bulgaria might unilaterally repudiate the contract by claiming it was signed corruptly by an unauthorized caretaker government, forcing it into arbitration. The resolution criteria clearly specify that unilateral termination, even if disputed by BOTAŞ, resolves the question as YES. I place the probability at 38%, balancing the intense domestic/EU political will to cancel the agreement against the massive financial and diplomatic deterrents to formal termination.
